subConcepts.name: Bronze Age collapse as catalyst
subConcepts.summary: The catastrophic collapse of Bronze Age civilization around 1177 BCE created a cognitive bottleneck that freed smaller societies to experiment with new psycho-technologies — the necessary precondition for the axial breakthrough.

passages.phrase: The Bronze Age collapses. There's some good books about this... What we know is that it's a collapse. Now you need to grasp the gravity of this collapse.

subConcepts.name: Alphabetic literacy and second-order thinking
subConcepts.summary: Alphabetic literacy, invented in Canaan and taken up by Hebrews and Greeks, dramatically increased cognitive fluency and enabled internalization of metacognition — producing Bellah's second-order thinking.

passages.phrase: Second order thinking is when you internalize a psycho-technology into your metacognition and it improves your capacity to critically examine your own thinking and correct your own thinking.

subConcepts.name: Coinage and abstract symbolic thought
subConcepts.summary: The invention of coinage trained people in abstract symbolic reasoning and numeracy, providing a second psycho-technological driver that combined with literacy to produce the axial cognitive revolution.

passages.phrase: money teaches you to think in an abstract symbol system. You start thinking in abstract symbol systems and it also teaches you something else. Numeracy.

subConcepts.name: Emergence of two-worlds mythology
subConcepts.summary: The axial revolution replaced the continuous cosmos with a two-worlds mythology in which the everyday world of illusion and self-deception is contrasted with the real world accessible to the trained, wise mind.

passages.phrase: this new mythological world view uses a mythology of two worlds. The idea is 'this' is the 'Everyday World'. This is the world of the untrained mind.

subConcepts.name: Awareness of self-deception and self-transcendence
subConcepts.summary: By combining second-order thinking with abstract symbolic thought, axial peoples gained unprecedented awareness of both their capacity for self-correction and their systematic vulnerability to self-deception.

passages.phrase: they start to become much more aware of the meaning making nature of their cognition, its capacity to generate illusion and self-deception and also its capacity to break out of illusion and self-deception and to come into a contact with a more real world.

subConcepts.name: Cross-cultural simultaneity of the revolution
subConcepts.summary: The axial revolution occurred independently in Greece, Israel, India, and China during roughly the same period, driven by comparable psycho-technological developments and shared pressures of post-Bronze-Age experimentation.

passages.phrase: around 800 BCE to around 300 B.C. there's this great change such that you will read, connect to, find relevant authors, systems of thought, ways of being from that time period.

subConcepts.name: The axial legacy in modernity
subConcepts.summary: The meaning crisis is partly a crisis of the axial legacy — the erosion of the psycho-technologies, two-worlds mythology, and nomological order that the axial revolution bequeathed to Western civilization.

passages.phrase: suffering from lack of meaning that is particularly pertinent for us today but has always been a perennial threat since the axial revolution.

commonConfusions: The Axial Revolution is not the same as the 'Axial Age' as a chronological label — Vervaeke uses 'Revolution' to mark the cognitive-cultural transformation driven by new psycho-technologies, not merely the time period identified by Jaspers.

The axial revolution is not a single event but a set of mutually reinforcing developments: alphabetic literacy, coinage, second-order thinking, and the two-worlds mythology all arose together as an interlocking system.

The axial revolution is not exclusively Western or Greek — Vervaeke insists it occurs simultaneously in India, China, and Israel, each producing culture-specific but functionally analogous psycho-technologies.
The axial revolution did not eliminate the continuous cosmos worldview; rather, the two-worlds mythology was layered on top of pre-axial cognition, and pre-axial elements persist in all of us.

keyPassages.phrase: Just like the Upper Paleolithic transition was formative of us as human beings, the Axial Age is formative of us as Western civilization. Or at least world civilization, because not just in the West that the Axial Revolution occurs. It also occurs in India and China.

This is the greatest collapse in civilization the world has ever known. The fall of the Roman Empire is nowhere near as devastating as this. More cities go out of existence at this time - the Bronze Age collapse - than any other time in recorded history.

bring that second order thinking and bring that abstract symbolic thought - more logically rigorous together - and what are you going to start getting. You're going to start getting people having a very clear sense of two things about their cognition.
The Axial revolution is this critical awareness and sense of responsibility of one's own cognition.
The core idea of rationality is your capacity for reflectively realizing your capacities for self-deception and illusion and for self correction.

subConcepts.name: Differences of power, not kind
subConcepts.summary: In the continuous cosmos, gods and humans differ only in degree of power and glory, not in fundamental nature — making the pharaoh's divinity non-metaphorical and human-divine overlap natural.

passages.phrase: the differences between human beings and the gods are differences in power. This is a cosmos where reality is experienced primarily in power, in terms of power. The gods are just more powerful than us, more glorious than us.

subConcepts.name: Cyclic time and nostalgia for origins
subConcepts.summary: Pre-axial time moves in great repeating cycles rather than as a linear story; ritual practice aims to re-tap the creative power of the cosmic origin rather than to move forward toward a promised future.

passages.phrase: It moves in great cycles. Just like the seasons. Just like day and night. Time moves in large cycles that repeat through eternity. In fact, what you're often trying to do with your ritual behavior is you're trying to tap into this continuity.

subConcepts.name: Deep continuity of natural, cultural, and divine worlds
subConcepts.summary: In the continuous cosmos there is no sharp boundary between nature, human culture, and the divine — animals may speak, humans may be gods, and the sacred pervades ordinary experience without requiring a separate realm.

passages.phrase: people feel there's a deep connection, a deep continuity, between the natural world and the cultural world. And between the cultural world and the world of the gods.

commonConfusions: The continuous cosmos is not a primitive or irrational worldview — it is internally coherent and plausible, and traces of it remain in modern cognition (the pharaoh's divinity was not a metaphor to ancient Egyptians).
The continuous cosmos is not simply identical to animism or pantheism — it is a structural feature of a worldview in which differences of kind between humans and gods have not yet emerged.

The axial revolution did not destroy the continuous cosmos; rather, the two-worlds mythology was overlaid on pre-axial sensibilities that persist in altered form — including, Vervaeke argues, in the scientific worldview's return to a 'cosmos without qualitative distinctions.'

keyPassages.phrase: So in the Bronze Age world, before the axial revolution, people experienced the world in what's been called by Charles Taylor 'the continuous cosmos'

It's not odd that animals might talk or they might have deep societies. It's not odd for us, for certain human individuals to think themselves divine like the great greatest pre Axial Age empire is Egypt. The Pharaoh is a god king!

your attitude towards the world is you want to fit into these cycles. You want to be in harmony with them. You don't want to really change things a lot because if I change this, if I change my future, I'm actually undermining my past.
Charles Taylor talks about this as 'The Great dis-embedding': when the Axial revolution hits, this world is replaced by a different one.

subConcepts.name: The everyday world as illusion
subConcepts.summary: The two-worlds mythology posits an everyday world beset by self-deception, illusion, and violence — not as an empirical claim but as a mythological characterization of untrained cognition.

passages.phrase: The idea is 'this' is the 'Everyday World'. This is the world of the untrained mind. This is a world that is beset by self-deception, self-destruction, illusion, violence, chaos.

subConcepts.name: The real world as accessible to wisdom
subConcepts.summary: The 'real world' in the two-worlds mythology is not a separate realm but the world as it appears to the trained, wise mind — the axial project is the process of moving from one to the other.

passages.phrase: the real world is how the trained mind, the wise mind, sees the world. This is how the world looks when you're in touch with reality.

commonConfusions: The two-worlds mythology is not a literal metaphysical claim about two separate ontological realms — Vervaeke insists it is mythological, a model for self-transformation, not an assertion that two dimensions of reality exist.

The two-worlds mythology is not identical across axial traditions — Greeks use the cave allegory, Buddhists use awakening from sleep, Hebrews use the journey from slavery to promised land; the structure is shared but the specific imagery varies.

The 'everyday world' in this mythology does not mean the physical world is unreal — it means untrained cognition systematically misframes reality through self-deception, and wisdom is what corrects that.

keyPassages.phrase: Now what's this new world? Well, this new mythological world view uses a mythology of two worlds. The idea is 'this' is the 'Everyday World'. This is the world of the untrained mind.
the real world is how the trained mind, the wise mind, sees the world. This is how the world looks when you're in touch with reality. When your mind is not beset by illusion and delusion.
It's not a reframing of a particular problem. It's a transformation of the whole framing process: Both ends.
Vervaeke insists this is mythological, not metaphysical: most axial-age thinkers used it as a model for self-transformation, not a literal claim about two realities.

subConcepts.name: Near-extinction bottleneck
subConcepts.summary: Before the Upper Paleolithic transition, humans nearly went extinct — possibly reduced to 10,000 individuals — and responded by developing broader trading networks and cognitive innovations rather than physical ones.

passages.phrase: they don't come up so much with a technological response because 'climate' is to huge and too poorly understood! They come up with a social cognitive response.

subConcepts.name: Software change, not hardware change
subConcepts.summary: The explosion of art, music, weapons, and symbolic behavior at 40,000 BCE was not driven by biological brain change but by a software-level reorganization of how an already-existing brain was being used — most plausibly through shamanism.

passages.phrase: It's not a hardware change. The brain has already existed for one hundred and sixty thousand years. It's not changing significantly during the upper Paeleolithic transition. It's much more likely that it's not a hardware change in the brain, it's a software change in how human beings are using the brain.

subConcepts.name: Explosion in human culture and technology
subConcepts.summary: The transition produced representational art, sculpture, cave paintings, music, calendars, and projectile weapons — all expressions of new forms of meaning-making that had no precursors.

passages.phrase: they're making art. They're making representational art. They're making sculpture. They're making cave paintings. We have good evidence they start making music.

commonConfusions: The Upper Paleolithic transition is not a single moment but a process — Vervaeke explicitly says there are precursors and a continuum, and picking a time only marks the most visible acceleration.
The transition is not explained by a genetic or brain-hardware change — Homo sapiens existed biologically for 160,000 years before 40,000 BCE; the change was cognitive-cultural, not biological.

Shamanism as a driver does not imply belief in supernatural powers — Vervaeke argues from cognitive science that shamanic practices are psycho-technologies that produce genuine cognitive enhancements through flow, altered states, and metaphorical cognition.

keyPassages.phrase: So this period is known as the Upper Paleolithic transition. It occurs around 40000 BCE. Now what's interesting about this is that biologically as a species we've existed much longer than this.

Notice how all of this is associated with different aspects of what we mean by meaning. Obviously there's art and there's music - that's somehow meaningful. But there's also time is being made more meaningful it's being measured and understood in calendars.

It's not a hardware change in the brain, it's a software change in how human beings are using the brain and what part of what I want to argue is that shamanism is probably playing a significant role in that software change.
Way before the Internet networked computers together, culture networked brains together in order to provide some of our most powerful problem solving abilities.

subConcepts.name: Disruptive strategies for insight
subConcepts.summary: Shamans use sleep deprivation, social isolation, extended chanting, dance, and sometimes psychedelics to disrupt normal framing — generating the de-automatization of cognition that enables insight cascades.

passages.phrase: The shaman will often engage in things like sleep deprivation. Intense long periods, hours of singing, dancing chanting. The shaman will often engage in immitation.

subConcepts.name: Participatory knowing via enactment
subConcepts.summary: By enacting the animal — becoming the deer in participatory knowing — the shaman gains insight-by-conformity that enhances hunting, healing, and community integration.

passages.phrase: the shaman is becoming the deer. I don't mean metaphysically, but the shaman is trying to get together the sense of the skills, the kind of perspective the deer has, the way the deer thinks, the kind of world the deer lives in.

subConcepts.name: Flow state and enhanced metaphorical cognition
subConcepts.summary: Shamanic practice cultivates the flow state, which produces implicit learning, insight cascades, and enhanced metaphorical cognition — connecting areas of the brain that normally do not communicate.

passages.phrase: the shaman is getting into the flow state; is developing all these techniques for getting into this deeply immersive, comprehensive, flow state and they're getting an insight cascade.

commonConfusions: Shamanism is not equivalent to supernaturalism — Vervaeke explicitly states he is seeking a naturalistic cognitive explanation of why shamanic practices produce real enhancements, not endorsing belief in spirits.

The shaman is not simply a charlatan or placebo effect generator — while the placebo effect is real and real (30–40%), shamanic efficacy also extends to genuine cognitive, social, and environmental problem-solving via flow and metaphorical cognition.
Shamanism in Vervaeke's usage is a set of psycho-technologies that can be scientifically analyzed — it is continuous with, not opposed to, the axial revolution's rational psycho-technologies.

keyPassages.phrase: Shamanism is a set of psycho-technologies for altering your state of consciousness and enhancing your cognition.
Notice what they're doing. They're really enhancing their capacity for cognition.
what's happening in this state is your brain is learning to get areas to talk to each other that normally don't talk to each other.

the shaman is developing psycho-technologies for altering the state of consciousness to get into the flow state and that flow state is already making them more insightful and more intuitively powerful.

subConcepts.name: Faith as participatory courseness
subConcepts.summary: In Hebraic thought, faith (emunah) is not belief in doctrines but the lived participatory sense of being on course — Da'ath — knowing one is moving in the right direction within the unfolding cosmic story.

passages.phrase: Faith was your sense of 'DA'ATH'. Faith was your sense that you are in this reciprocal realization: you're 'in course'. You're 'on course'. You're involved and evolving with things.

subConcepts.name: Sin as going off course
subConcepts.summary: Sin in the Hebraic tradition means missing the mark — being self-deceived and thereby navigating off the course of the unfolding cosmic story toward the promised land.

passages.phrase: So the idea here is I'm trying to sense the course of things and, if I'm self deluded or illusory, I'm actually dramatically off course without realizing it.

commonConfusions: Da'ath is not propositional knowledge about God — it is participatory knowing of being in the right relational course, the same word used for sexual intercourse, marking it as a deeply intimate, mutual knowing.

Faith in the Hebraic sense derived from Da'ath is not credulous assertion of beliefs without evidence — that modern meaning is recent and largely a product of the Reformation's reduction of faith to propositional assent.

Da'ath is not a uniquely religious concept in Vervaeke's usage — it describes the same participatory-knowing structure that appears in loving relationships, expert practice, and any situation where one senses 'I'm on course.'

keyPassages.phrase: Faith was your sense of 'DA'ATH'. Faith was your sense that you are in this reciprocal realization: you're 'in course'. You're 'on course'. You're involved and evolving with things.

So people sometimes note, and often humorously, that the Bible will talk about sex - sexual intercourse - with the verb 'knowing', so you'll get things like 'Adam knew his wife Eve'... Its 'DA'ATH' in this sense.
Notice you have this in your relationships. You'll often be asking yourself 'How is this relationship going? Is it on course? Is it progressing? Is it growing?'
Knowing the fullness of time, knowing exactly the right time. The right timing to shift the course of events. What Pascal, when we come to Pascal, we'll talk about is 'The Spirit of Finesse'.

subConcepts.name: Kairos within narrative order
subConcepts.summary: In the Hebraic tradition, Kairos moments are the turning points in the cosmic narrative at which God intervenes to redirect the course of history — and prophets are those who identify and proclaim these moments.

passages.phrase: God intervenes at moments of KAIROS - turning points where he tries to bring people back on course.

subConcepts.name: Kairos personalized in Christianity
subConcepts.summary: Christianity radicalizes Kairos by claiming that the ultimate cosmic turning point is incarnated in a particular person — Jesus — making it possible for each individual to experience a personal Kairos through encounter with him.

passages.phrase: Jesus of Nazareth is actually the ultimate Kairos. That all the other Kairos were pointing to him and are summed up in him. That he represents the ultimate turning point.

commonConfusions: Kairos is not the same as chronos — Vervaeke distinguishes the linear sequential flow of time (chronos) from the qualitative right moment of intervention (kairos) that is participatorily known, not calculated.

The prophet who identifies Kairos is not a fortune-teller — prophecy means 'telling forth' (waking people up to how they are off course right now), not predicting future events, and the therapist is a better modern analogy than the psychic.
Kairos is not only a religious concept in Vervaeke's framework — it reappears in Relevance Realization as temporality (centrality in time), making it a structural feature of all meaningful cognition.

keyPassages.phrase: Now, there's a technical term for these turning points: Kairos. It's developed by the theologian Paul Tulloch. This is this sense of the crucial turning point.

When you're making love with somebody you are participating in them. You're identifying with them, empathizing with them, resonating with them. You are changing them as they are changing you and it - Forgive me the pun - to a climax! To a turning point to a resolution.
Jesus of Nazareth is actually the ultimate Kairos. That all the other Kairos were pointing to him and are summed up in him.
And so the idea is God has to intervene periodically. God has to redeem. God has to do something to wake people up. To remind them. To help them to sense how they've gone off course.

subConcepts.name: Integration of Socratic and Pythagorean strands
subConcepts.summary: Plato synthesizes Socrates's participatory self-knowledge (know thyself) with Pythagoras's self-transcendence through rational pattern-discovery, producing a unified program of inner ordering and outward contact with reality.

passages.phrase: Plato describes wisdom as an internal Justice within the psyche in which the man has been taught, the line has been trained and the monster has been tamed so they can get along together as much as is possible.

subConcepts.name: Reduction of inner conflict as wisdom
subConcepts.summary: For Plato, wisdom is achieved when reason (man) trains spirit/thymos (lion) and tames appetite (monster), reducing inner conflict and allowing salience, understanding, and participation to come into alignment.

passages.phrase: What you want to do is get it properly ordered. When you don't order it, think about what this means... Salience and understanding and participation get out of sync with each other.

subConcepts.name: Anagogic mutual deepening of self and reality
subConcepts.summary: As inner conflict is reduced the person comes into better contact with reality, and as they come into better contact with reality they can further reduce inner conflict — a mutually reinforcing anagogic cycle.

passages.phrase: I improve a little bit; my skill at picking up on real patterns. I use that skill on myself to increase my self-knowledge and get better patterns, which means I reduce my inner conflict. As my inner conflict goes down, I get a clearer vision of reality.

commonConfusions: The Platonic project is not simply idealism — Vervaeke stresses that the forms (idos) are patterns both in the mind and in the world, and that knowing them is a participatory contact epistemology, not an exercise in conceptual abstraction.

Plato's myth of the cave is not a claim that the physical world is unreal — it is a parable about the difference between untrained and trained cognition, illustrating that wisdom is a process of increasingly accurate participatory contact with reality.

The tripartite psyche (man, lion, monster) is not a primitive psychology that neuroscience has superseded — Vervaeke argues it keeps being rediscovered (Freud's id/ego/superego; triune-brain theory) because Plato identified something real about motivational architecture.

keyPassages.phrase: Plato describes wisdom as an internal Justice within the psyche in which the man has been taught, the line has been trained and the monster has been tamed so they can get along together as much as is possible.
This (Myth of the Cave) is a myth of enlightenment: of coming into the light. It's a myth of self-transcendence and self-transformation.
As I get better knowledge of myself, I can better teach the man. To be a good teacher, you have to know your student better.
The core idea of rationality is your capacity for reflectively realizing your capacities for self-deception and illusion and for self correction.

subConcepts.name: Shadows as merely correlational patterns
subConcepts.summary: The chained prisoners mistake correlational patterns (shadows) for reality — an image of how untrained cognition latches onto salient but non-explanatory patterns while remaining unable to see the real structural organization behind them.

passages.phrase: people take the shadows and the echoes to be the real things because they're chained. They're caught up.

subConcepts.name: Transformative ascent through repeated blindness
subConcepts.summary: The freed prisoner is repeatedly blinded as they ascend — each new level of reality exceeds current perceptual capacity and requires self-transformation before the next step becomes possible, illustrating anagoge.

passages.phrase: When they take a step forward, they're blinded by the light. And they have to wait. They have to wait for their eyes to adjust. The self has to be transformed.

commonConfusions: The myth of the cave is not a claim that the material world is unreal — it is a parable about cognitive development, in which 'shadows' represent the salient but shallow patterns of untrained minds, not the unreality of matter.

Enlightenment in the cave myth is not an Eastern concept imported into Western philosophy — Vervaeke explicitly notes this is a myth of enlightenment in the Western tradition, and the same structure appears across axial cultures.

keyPassages.phrase: This (Myth of the Cave) is a myth of enlightenment: of coming into the light. It's a myth of self-transcendence and self-transformation.

And of course what they do is they go back down into the tunnel rapidly, and they get here, and they try to tell their fellow prisoners what they saw. But of course they're stumbling around because their eyes don't work anymore in that darkness.
There's a Greek word for this 'ascent' called 'Anagoge'. This is the 'Anagogic', or the 'Anagogical' aspect of Plato's idea.

What's Plato's myth? ...There's people chained to chairs so all they can do is look at the back of the cave ...Then there's other people walking in front of the fire and it's casting shadows onto the cave because of the firelight.

subConcepts.name: Knowing as contact, not description
subConcepts.summary: Aristotle's conformity theory treats knowing as an intimate contact with a thing — taking on the same structural-functional organization — rather than as standing apart and generating accurate propositions about it.

passages.phrase: So to know something is to be in contact with it, is to actually participate in the same form as the thing. I'm going to come back to this sense of participatory knowing.

subConcepts.name: Knowing changes the knower's being
subConcepts.summary: Because conformity theory requires taking on the same form as what is known, genuine knowing involves a change in the knower's very being — not just a change in beliefs — making knowing and becoming inseparable.

passages.phrase: I'm actually changing my structure. This isn't just... I'm not just knowing and having beliefs, I'm being changed. This is a change in my being, not just a change in my knowing.

commonConfusions: The conformity theory does not claim that knowledge is merely subjective or relativistic — conforming to the real pattern in the thing means getting at what is actually there, not just projecting a mental image onto it.

Aristotle's conformity theory is not the same as the modern correspondence theory of truth — correspondence asks whether propositions match facts, whereas conformity asks whether the mind takes on the same structural-functional organization as the thing itself.

Conformity theory is not a premodern curiosity — Vervaeke argues it is undergoing a revival in 4E cognitive science (Dreyfus, Merleau-Ponty, enactivism), which vindicates Aristotle's contact epistemology.

keyPassages.phrase: when I know some object or know some thing, my mind takes on the same structural functional organization as the thing such that if I could take that idos from my mind and actualize it in some potential, I could make an instance of the thing

I can actually conform (picks cup up) to the cup. I'm actually taking the same shape! And notice how this enables me to causally interact with the cup in a much more intimate and complex and sophisticated fashion.
The conformity theory doesn't just change your beliefs it changes the very structure and functioning of your being.

The conformity theory is a very different way of thinking about how we know things. So Charles Taylor, who I've mentioned before, Hubert Dreyfus and others, they talk about the Conformity Theory as a 'contact' epistemology.

commonConfusions: The shift from conformity to correspondence theory is not simply scientific progress — Vervaeke argues it is a loss, because the correspondence theory strips out participatory and perspectival knowing, leaving only propositional knowing.

The correspondence theory is not wrong about what it does describe — it correctly captures one kind of knowing (propositional), but treating it as the only kind of knowing is what drives the meaning crisis.
Recovering conformity theory does not mean abandoning science — Vervaeke argues 4E cognitive science is already vindicating contact epistemology within a rigorous scientific framework.

keyPassages.phrase: Aristotle's theory of knowing there is no distinction - that is that we would typically have - between knowing and being.

Here's the modern view (cup at distance): I'm over here describing it; it's over there, independent. I'm over here describing it. Here's Aristotle's view (picks cup up): I'm actually changing my structure.

The conformity theory is a very different way of thinking about how we know things. So Charles Taylor, who I've mentioned before, Hubert Dreyfus and others, they talk about the Conformity Theory as a 'contact' epistemology.

And so Pascal is pointing out that what has been lost in the scientific revolution is all these other kinds of knowing and being, and these are the kinds of knowing and being that he found present in the transformative experience that he had.

subConcepts.name: Logos as structural-functional organization
subConcepts.summary: In its original Greek sense, logos names the intelligible pattern — the structural-functional organization — that makes something be what it is and that the mind can take on in knowing it.

passages.phrase: The Germans have a great word for this - 'Gestalt'. In English we don't! The Greeks have a good word for this (S-FO): the word 'logos'.

subConcepts.name: Logos in the Gospel of John
subConcepts.summary: The Gospel of John appropriates the Stoic and Greek concept of logos to identify God's creative intelligibility principle with a particular person — Jesus — making Kairos and logos coincide in a historical individual.

passages.phrase: Christianity... it's in the gospel of John 'Anarchaum Logos', 'In the beginning was the Logos'... God's capacity for producing Kairos through Logos has been identified, or to use an older term 'incarnated', in a particular individual.

commonConfusions: Logos does not mean 'word' in the ordinary sense — it means the intelligible structural-functional organization (what makes a bird a bird), and its use as 'word' in the Gospel of John carries the full freight of its Greek philosophical sense.

Logos and mythos are not opposites in Vervaeke's account — they are complementary; logos gives the rational articulation of what mythos enacts participatorily, and their severance in modernity is a driver of the meaning crisis.

keyPassages.phrase: The Greeks have a good word for this (S-FO): the word 'logos', although a word that's being discussed a lot today. I think it needs to be discussed a little bit more carefully.
this logos, this real pattern, is not only how you know something but it's also the pattern that makes it be what it is.
Logos doesn't mean just spoken words. It means like the intelligibility, the formative principle, the underlying structure.
That form, that logos, is not only how the thing is integrated together, it's how your mind can be integrated with it.

commonConfusions: Akrasia is not simply weakness of will in the Protestant sense — Vervaeke rejects the notion that 'willpower' is what is lacking; Aristotle's point is that character (habituated disposition) is what is deficient, not a mental muscle called will.

Akrasia does not imply that knowledge is irrelevant to virtue — it refutes Socratic intellectualism (that knowledge of the good entails doing it), but Aristotle's response is not to abandon reason but to supplement it with cultivated character.

Akrasia is not weakness in the face of temptation in a moral sense — it is a cognitive-developmental failure: the structural-functional organization of character has not been cultivated so that salience and understanding align.

keyPassages.phrase: Akrasia is when you know what the right thing to do is - you KNOW what the right thing to do is - and we talked about this, remember, with the chocolate cake. But you don't do the right thing.

which we poorly translate as 'weakness of the will' because we're all post Protestants and we think the will is our central thing even though, increasingly, there's scientific evidence that the notion of will or willpower is a defunct idea, we should give it up!

I know I should lose weight. There is the evidence, it's clear and makes rational sense to me. I should lose weight. Yes yes. OK. I should lose weight. And then I go home and there sitting on the counter is some chocolate cake!
Ignorance is when you do the wrong thing because you don't know. Part of what foolishness is, is when you know what the right thing is and you still do the wrong thing.

commonConfusions: Absurdity in Vervaeke's sense is not primarily a philosophical thesis (Camus's absurdism) but a phenomenological diagnosis: the lived experience of agent-arena decoupling, when one's existential mode no longer affords meaningful action.

Absurdity is not the same as meaninglessness in a nihilistic sense — it is specifically the mismatch between agent and arena (the tennis player in the football field), not a claim that meaning is impossible in principle.

The cure for absurdity is not intellectual insight alone — because absurdity is an existential mode, resolving it requires transframing (a transformation of both self and world), not simply adopting a new philosophy.

keyPassages.phrase: You will be like the tennis player trying to play tennis in the football arena. You will start to experience your existence as absurd. It won't make any sense to you.
If I put the tennis player into the football arena, it's absurd! It doesn't make any sense! Things aren't... the tennis player can do all they want and it doesn't make any sense.
one of the ways in which the meaning crisis expresses itself is in people saying that they feel existence is absurd.
As the Nomological order breaks down, of course, then we start to confront absurdity and we start to lose a sense of how we fit in and how we belong.

subConcepts.name: Grand unification of Platonic, Aristotelian, and Stoic strands
subConcepts.summary: Plotinus synthesizes Plato's spirituality and anagoge, Aristotle's worldview and conformity theory, and Stoic therapeutic practice into a single integrated system in which science, therapy, and spirituality are mutually supporting.

passages.phrase: Plotinus is the grand unified field theory of ancient spirituality because what does he do? Well, he takes Plato's spirituality. He takes the whole idea of anagoge from Plato. He takes Aristotle's theory, the whole Aristotle worldview, the theory of knowing, the theory of the structure of the world.

subConcepts.name: Levels of reality and levels of self ascending together
subConcepts.summary: In Neoplatonism, as one conforms to progressively more integrated levels of reality, one simultaneously ascends to progressively more integrated levels of self — reality and self co-deepen anagogically.

passages.phrase: As we make these levels of reality viable to us as they become livable to us, we conform to them and we change... As we conform, we're also altering, and this is the anagoge of Plato, we're altering what level the self is at.

subConcepts.name: The One as inexhaustible apex
subConcepts.summary: The apex of the Neoplatonic system is The One — that by which everything is real and everything is known — which cannot be known propositionally but can be reached through gnosis, being-at-one with it.

passages.phrase: Plotinus calls this The One. He doesn't mean the single. This is that by which reality is realized and our mind realizes reality.

commonConfusions: Neoplatonism is not simply a religious movement — it is an integrated system of science, therapy, and spirituality that Vervaeke regards as the paradigm of their proper integration, against which their modern separation looks like a loss.

Plotinus's The One is not identical to the Christian God — it is impersonal, beyond all predication, and known only through a higher state of consciousness; Augustine's synthesis of Neoplatonism and Christianity required significant transformation of both.

Neoplatonism's levels of reality are not a literal ontological hierarchy — they are best understood as levels of integration and realness that both the world and the self ascend through in a participatory anagogic process.

keyPassages.phrase: Plotinus is the grand unified field theory of ancient spirituality because what does he do? Well, he takes Plato's spirituality. He takes the whole idea of anagoge from Plato. He takes Aristotle's theory, the whole Aristotle worldview.

There is no deep division in Plotinus between spirituality, science, and therapy. They are all beautifully, mutually supporting, interwoven, enmeshed together. It's the culmination of everything from the Greek axial age.
You can't know it. You can't have it. You can't have it even in though. You can't have it even in the most beautiful theory. You can't have it. You can only be it.
What makes something real is how one it is. How integrated it is. How much it is structurally, functionally organized.

commonConfusions: The One is not singular in the sense of one-among-many — Vervaeke is explicit that Plotinus does not mean 'the single'; The One is the principle by which all things are realized and by which all minds realize things, beyond enumeration.

The One cannot be known propositionally — because all propositional knowledge involves distinctions and The One is prior to all distinctions, it can only be 'known' by being one, i.e., through a higher state of consciousness.

The One is not identical to the Christian God — it has no will, no intentionality, no personal relationship; Augustine had to significantly transform both Neoplatonism and Christian theology to integrate them.

keyPassages.phrase: Plotinus calls this The One. He doesn't mean the single. This is that by which reality is realized and our mind realizes reality.
Plotinus uses the metaphor of light. Just like light is itself invisible and makes everything seeable, The One is not anything we can ever know.
You can only have gnosis of The One by being one.

From Plotinus himself, we get now what I'm going to call the Normative Order. Plotinus gave us an account of how we can move in a coordinated fashion up the levels of reality, up the levels of consciousness, up the levels of the self, from what is less real to what is more real.

subConcepts.name: Gnosticism as the axial revolution within the axial revolution
subConcepts.summary: Gnosticism radicalizes the axial revolution by applying the two-worlds mythology even to the gods — the gods themselves are part of the illusory trap, and transcendence must go beyond all divine authority.

passages.phrase: I'm presenting the Gnostic movement as the axial revolution within the axial revolution.

subConcepts.name: Gnostic mythology as scaffolding for gnosis
subConcepts.summary: The bizarre and elaborate Gnostic mythological narratives are not intended as literal cosmology — they are scaffolding for the participatory knowing of gnosis, the transformative release from existential entrapment.

passages.phrase: what I want to try and make clear to you from the inside, so to speak, what Gnosis is and how you are probably at some point seeking it, or will be seeking it, or have sought it in your life.

subConcepts.name: The dark side: conspiracy and antisemitism
subConcepts.summary: Gnosticism's tendency to see the everyday world as a prison built by evil powers can collapse into conspiracy theory and has historically fed into deeply antisemitic forms of thought — which is why Vervaeke wants to salvage gnosis without gnosticism.

passages.phrase: This is ultimately a conspiracy theory. This is a grand conspiracy theory that behind — Look, one of the reasons why this is comforting is, behind all the apparent chaos and suffering, there actually is some evil overlord.

subConcepts.name: Extricating gnosis from gnosticism
subConcepts.summary: Vervaeke's constructive response to the Gnostic dark side is to extricate the participatory knowing (gnosis) from the historical movement (gnosticism), salvaging the transformative insight while refusing the conspiracy framework.

passages.phrase: In order to understand it, we must think of how we can extricate gnosis from Gnosticism. How can we salvage gnosis from Gnosticism? Just like, how can we salvage the agape from Christianity? How can we salvage wisdom from the ancient nomological order?

commonConfusions: Gnosticism is not a single organized religion — Vervaeke presents it as a style or movement (like existentialism or fundamentalism) that pervades early Christianity without constituting a distinct community.

Gnosis (the transformative knowing) and gnosticism (the historical movement with its mythology) must be distinguished — Vervaeke wants to recover the former while being critical of the latter's tendency toward conspiracy and antisemitism.

The Gnostic mythology, however bizarre, is not meant literally — it is a mythological scaffolding for the existential experience of feeling trapped and seeking liberation through participatory knowing.

keyPassages.phrase: I'm presenting the Gnostic movement as the axial revolution within the axial revolution. It's taking the revolution to its culmination in many important ways that I think have direct relevance for us today.

The Gnostics want to invert that. They want to tell you that instead of worshiping these gods, instead of idealizing these gods, instead of giving in to these powers and principalities... we have to transcend them all.

gnosticism as Hans Jonas and other people have seen is directly relevant to a lot of the modern confrontation with meaninglessness and nihilism because in the meaning crisis, people also similarly feel deeply, existentially trapped.

the Gnostics have a very, and this is one of the things their critics often leveled against them. The Gnostics keep churning out new gospels and new stories and new myths. Because for them it's not about having a final Orthodox story.

subConcepts.name: Hellenistic domicide and age of anxiety
subConcepts.summary: The Hellenistic period created cultural domicide on a massive scale — people were uprooted from polis, language group, shared religion, and history by Alexander's conquests — producing the age of anxiety that generated Stoicism, Epicureanism, and early Christianity.

passages.phrase: You are now probably thousands of miles away from the seat of government. You do not participate in that government, nor do you know, personally, most of the people or any of the people in it.

subConcepts.name: Physical versus cultural domicide
subConcepts.summary: Cultural domicide — having a house but not a home — is the deeper form: the loss of connectedness to place, culture, history, language, and community that constitutes identity, even while the physical dwelling remains intact.

passages.phrase: Domicide is the destruction of home. Now there's two ways in which Domicide can occur. One, of course, is physical destruction of your house, and that's important, but there's also Cultural Domicide in which you have a house, you have a dwelling, but it is not very much your home.

commonConfusions: Domicide is not simply homesickness or nostalgia — it is the systematic destruction of the agent-arena relationship at the level of culture and community, making individual meaning systems unworkable.

Domicide is not confined to the Hellenistic period — Vervaeke explicitly uses it as a bridge concept between ancient and modern meaning crises, arguing that our current situation involves analogous cultural un-homing.

The loss of home in domicide is not primarily about geography — it is the loss of the relational, cultural, and historical embeddedness in which identity is constituted and meaning-making is made possible.

keyPassages.phrase: Domicide is the destruction of home. Now there's two ways in which Domicide can occur. One, of course, is physical destruction of your house, and that's important, but there's also Cultural Domicide in which you have a house, you have a dwelling, but it is not very much your home.
People are experiencing this radical sense of Domicide. They don't have deep connections to themselves, to each other, to their environment, to their history, to their cultural surroundings.
notice how often we will use the language of loss of home to describe our current situation where we often talk about how we now feel un-homed in the cosmos.
one of the functions of sacredness is what Geertz called a Meta-Meaning Function... religion isn't a system of meaning... He thinks it's a system of Meta-Meaning.

commonConfusions: Dukkha does not mean 'all is pain' or 'life is suffering' — Vervaeke insists this is a Western mistranslation; the actual meaning is that all life is threatened with loss of agency, self-destructive disjointedness, and entrapment.

The Buddha's claim is not pessimistic — it is a provocation to awareness: realize that all your life is threatened with dukkha so you take the eightfold path seriously, not a claim that nothing is enjoyable.

Dukkha is not caused simply by desire in the sense of wanting things — it is caused by a pattern of attachment analogous to addiction: a parasitic processing and reciprocal narrowing that eliminates alternatives.

keyPassages.phrase: What does Dukkha mean? Well the etymology is: imagine you have a wheel, and it's off-center on its axis, so the axle is not properly going through the center of the wheel and as the wheel is turning it's destroying itself.
all of your life is threatened, very really threatened, existentially threatened, by a capacity for self-destructive behavior. Self-deceptive, self-destructive behavior.
To 'suffer' means to undergo. It means to lose Agency. So you can actually suffer Joy.
The Buddha is not saying everything's painful. That's ridiculous. Because if everything was painful nothing would be painful.

commonConfusions: Nihilism is not merely a philosophical position one can choose to adopt or reject — Vervaeke presents it as a cognitive-cultural pathology that arises historically when the machinery of meaning (nomological, normative, narrative order) breaks down.

Schopenhauer's nihilism is not the same as Nietzsche's nihilism — Schopenhauer is pessimistic (the will to live is meaningless), while Nietzsche proposes a response (will to power and self-transcendence), though Vervaeke argues Nietzsche's response ultimately fails.

Nihilism is not resolved by simply asserting meaning — because it is a pathology of the relevance realization machinery and worldview collapse, it requires the re-construction of nomological, normative, and narrative order.

keyPassages.phrase: So here Romanticism very quickly passes into Schopenhauer (writes Schopenhauer on the board) who is the godfather of Nihilism
So for Schopenhauer it's this nihilism, it's this pessimism because he saw that once you remove the connection between Meaning Making and Rationality, you pay a very, very devastating price for it.
Romanticism and Nihilism, are actually deeply intertwined and closely related to each other. So think about that when Valentine's Day rolls around!
the history of metaphysics, that whole philosophical history coming out of the axial revolution is actually the history of nihilism.

commonConfusions: Philosophy is not originally about propositional argumentation to victory — Vervaeke stresses Pythagoras invented the word as 'friendship-love of wisdom' (philia-sophia), a communal and aspirational practice, and Plato repeatedly contrasts it with philia-nikia (love of victory).

Philia-sophia is not the same as academic philosophy — the modern discipline has, in Vervaeke's account, largely collapsed into philia-nikia: competitive debate aimed at defeating opponents rather than cooperative transformation toward wisdom.

Loving wisdom is not the same as possessing it — the philosopher is defined by aspiration toward wisdom, not by having achieved it, and this is why Socrates's constant profession of ignorance is philosophically correct.

keyPassages.phrase: Pythagoras actually invents the word 'Philosophy'. It comes from two Greek words: 'Philia' and 'Sophia'.

This means 'the friendship love of wisdom'. So, Pythagoras creates a community around him. You create a community - distributed cognition - in which you interact with other people in order to try and pursue wisdom.
Plato consistently makes a distinction between philia sophia, right? The love of wisdom. And philia nikia. The love of victory.
The love of victory. The having of the correct answer that defeats the opponent. And one of Plato's ongoing points is this is the deepest kind of bullshitting.

commonConfusions: The spirit of finesse is not mere intuition or feeling — it is the integrated exercise of procedural, perspectival, and participatory knowing that the spirit of geometry (mathematical-computational knowing) cannot capture.

Pascal's concern is not anti-scientific — he himself was a major figure of the scientific revolution; his argument is that the spirit of geometry, when treated as the only valid form of knowing, eliminates the kinds of knowing needed for existential transformation.

Losing the spirit of finesse is not simply an aesthetic or cultural loss — it is a cognitive loss that undermines the possibility of metanoia, gnosis, and the kind of transformative truth that wisdom requires.

keyPassages.phrase: Pascal makes a distinction between what he calls the Spirit of Geometry... and what he calls the Spirit of Finesse. And his fear, his concern is that we have lost this, and we have come to think of all of knowing and being in terms of the spirit of geometry.

we have slowly lost the importance of procedural knowledge - knowing how to do things; we've lost perspectival knowing - knowing what it's like; and we've lost participatory knowing - knowing that is part and parcel of how we are bound up with something else.

And so Pascal is pointing out that what has been lost in the scientific revolution is all these other kinds of knowing and being, and these are the kinds of knowing and being that he found present in the transformative experience that he had.
To do something with finesse... if I'm doing a move in Thai chi and I do it with finesse, It's like jazz! There's an element in there that I can't capture in terms of mathematical propositions.

subConcepts.name: Historical drivers of the meaning crisis
subConcepts.summary: The meaning crisis has specific historical causes: the Protestant Reformation, the scientific revolution, and modernity's erosion of the axial-age legacy have dismantled the nomological, normative, and narrative orders that Western civilization used to generate meaning.

passages.phrase: these problems are deeper than just social media problems, political problems, even economic problems. They're deeply historical, cultural, cognitive problems.

subConcepts.name: Perennial dimension of the meaning crisis
subConcepts.summary: Beyond historical factors, the meaning crisis has a perennial dimension: the same relevance-realization machinery that makes humans adaptive also generates endemic vulnerability to self-deception, modal confusion, and existential entrapment.

passages.phrase: the very same machinery that makes you so adaptive intelligent is the same machinery that makes you susceptible to foolishness.

subConcepts.name: Symptomatology: disconnection, nihilism, bullshit
subConcepts.summary: The meaning crisis manifests as rising rates of mental illness, suicide, nihilism, cynicism, erosion of institutional trust, loss of religious affiliation, and a pervading sense of drowning in bullshit — symptoms that share a common cognitive-historical root.

passages.phrase: is this increasing sense of bullshit. Bullshit is on the increase. It's more and more pervasive throughout our lives and there's this sense of drowning in this old ocean of bullshit.

subConcepts.name: Three interlocking crises
subConcepts.summary: The meaning crisis does not stand alone but is deeply interdependent with the environmental crisis and the socioeconomic crisis — understanding and addressing any one of them requires understanding their mutual entanglement.

passages.phrase: the three crises are not independent from each other. They're interdependent in important ways.

commonConfusions: The meaning crisis is not caused solely by social media, political dysfunction, or economic inequality — Vervaeke argues these are symptoms or co-drivers of a deeper cognitive-historical-cultural crisis rooted in the erosion of axial-age meaning-making.

The meaning crisis is not a crisis of belief — it is a crisis of religio (participatory binding to the world) and of the practices that cultivated it; reducing it to a loss of religious doctrine misses the cognitive depth of the problem.

Solving the meaning crisis does not require returning to traditional religion — the response Vervaeke proposes is a religion that is not a religion: a naturalistic, scientific worldview-embedded ecology of practices that re-engages religio without supernatural metaphysical commitments.

The meaning crisis is not uniquely modern — it has a perennial dimension (the endemic vulnerability to self-deception) that makes it a persistent threat across cultures; what is modern is the removal of the historical safeguards against it.

keyPassages.phrase: This is gonna be an idea that our culture is experiencing a profound 'meaning crisis'. A 'crisis in meaning'.
today there is an increase of people feeling very disconnected from themselves, from each other, from the world, from a viable and foreseeable future.
the meaning crisis is interacting with other crisies: the environmental crisis. The socio-economic crisis.
What is this 'meaning'? Why do we hunger for it? And how do we cultivate the wisdom to realize it?
since the axial revolution it is just crucially connected to the project of meaning in life.

commonConfusions: Vervaeke uses 'prophet' in the Old Testament sense — not as one who predicts the future but as one who tells forth: who wakes us up to how we are off course — making Nietzsche, Heidegger, Tillich, Jung, Corbin, and Barfield prophets in this technical sense.

The prophets of the meaning crisis are not in agreement — they offer different and sometimes opposed responses, and Vervaeke's project is to put them into constructive dialogue with his cognitive-science framework rather than to endorse any one of them as definitive.

Being a prophet of the meaning crisis does not make these thinkers exempt from critique — Vervaeke criticizes Nietzsche's inability to address self-deception, Heidegger's political failures, and others; the goal is dialogue, not deference.

keyPassages.phrase: This is ultimately going to come back to the dialogue that I'm going to set up between what I've argued for here and some of the other profits of this religion beyond a religion, that's not a religion like, Tillich, Jung — I would argue, also Corbin and perhaps aspects of Barfield, and the godfather of all of that, of course, are people like, Heidegger.
I want to put this into dialogue. I hope like I said, constructive dialogue with some of the central prophets of the meaning crisis, especially in the 20th and 21st century.
Prophecy means 'a telling fourth'. The job of the prophet is to wake you up right now to how you are off course.
the framework we have built allows us to enter into deep dialogue with the central prophets of the meaning crisis in a way that I think insightfully discloses aspects of their own theorizing.
